narrative: As a white male applicant with a solid middle-income family background, your law school application stood out in some ways, like your LSAT score of 38, which is above the typical 36.7 average, and your Year 1 school decile of 6, slightly better than the average of 5.71. However, the admissions model, which predicts the likelihood of passing the bar exam on the first try, estimated a 57.8% chance that you would fail it—high enough to trigger a rejection, as the school prioritizes applicants predicted to succeed. This prediction hinged on just a few key factors from your profile, with your undergraduate GPA, Year 3 school decile, and LSAT score being the most influential. Your LSAT was the strongest positive signal, pushing the model toward predicting you'd pass because it's a top indicator of bar success for students like you. On the flip side, your Year 3 decile of 4—below the average of 5.53—had a notable pull in the opposite direction, signaling to the model a dip in later academic performance that often correlates with bar struggles. Similarly, your undergrad GPA of 2.7, lower than the typical 3.21, reinforced concerns about sustaining the high performance needed for the bar. Compared to typical students who get approved, your profile shows strengths in early grades and test-taking but lags in later consistency and overall undergrad record, tipping the balance toward failure risk. Being full-time aligns with most applicants at 92.7%, so it didn't sway things much. While this rejection stings, especially with your promising LSAT, it's not the end—many in similar situations retake undergrad-level courses or boost grad metrics to shift predictions. Consider targeted prep to improve your later academic deciles, perhaps through a post-bac program, and reapply once you've addressed these gaps. Admissions models evolve, and demonstrating upward trends could flip your odds next time. You've got the foundational pieces; building on them will open doors.
